1: Abraham had taken another wife, whose name was Keturah — the mention of Abraham’s second wife (or concubine; cf. 1 Chronicles 1:32) and her sons is the genealogical prologue that situates Isaac as the singular covenant heir among many natural sons. Abraham’s extensive family through Keturah demonstrates that the covenant narrowing to Isaac is not from lack of other options but from divine election. Paul’s argument in Romans 9:6–13 is precisely this: not all of Abraham’s descendants are the heirs of the promise. The covenant-heir is determined by God’s call, not by birth order or number — the logic that leads to Christ as the one seed through whom all Abraham’s true offspring inherit (Galatians 3:16, 29).
2: Keturah bore Abraham Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak and Shuah — of these, Midian is the most significant canonically. Moses flees to Midian (Exodus 2:15), marries Zipporah the Midianite, and encounters God at the burning bush in Midian (Exodus 3:1). The Midianites later become a source of apostasy for Israel (Numbers 25:6; Genesis 31:1–18) and oppression (Judg 6–8). The Midianite connection — through Abraham’s secondary family — is a reminder that God works in and through even the non-covenant lines to accomplish his purposes (as with Jethro, Moses’s father-in-law, a Midianite priest who worships the LORD rightly in Exodus 18:10–12).
3: Jokshan was the father of Sheba and Dedan — the sons of Jokshan trace to the Arabian peninsula. Sheba appears in the OT as the land from which the Queen of Sheba travels to visit Solomon (1 Kings 10:1–13). Jesus uses this visit as a type of the Gentile response to divine wisdom: “The Queen of the South will rise at the judgment with this generation and condemn it; for she came from the ends of the earth to listen to Solomon’s wisdom, and now something greater than Solomon is here” (Matthew 12:42). The Sheba connection, traced through Abraham’s secondary family, is a canonical-context note on the far-reaching scope of Abraham’s genealogical influence.
4: The sons of Midian were Ephah, Epher, Hanok, Abida and Eldaah — Isaiah 60:6 mentions the Midianites (Ephah specifically) among the nations who will bring gold and incense to honor the LORD at the time of Israel’s eschatological restoration: “Herds of camels will cover your land, young camels of Midian and Ephah.” The Magi who bring gold and frankincense to the infant Jesus (Matthew 2:11) fulfill this vision. The Midianite offspring of Abraham become, in Isaiah’s vision, part of the Gentile procession to the restored Jerusalem — which the Magi’s visit anticipates. Abraham’s secondary offspring are within the scope of the eschatological gathering.
5: Abraham left everything he owned to Isaac — the sole heirship of Isaac over all Abraham’s estate is the clearest legal statement of the covenant’s singular channel. The NT applies the same singular-heir logic to Christ: “in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son, whom he appointed heir of all things” (Hebrews 1:2). Christ is the Isaac who inherits everything; believers are “co-heirs with Christ” (Romans 8:17) — they inherit by virtue of their union with the one heir, not by independent right. The entire Abrahamic estate passed to Isaac; the entire divine inheritance is held by Christ and shared with those who are in him.
6: While still living, Abraham gave gifts to the sons of his concubines and sent them east, away from his son Isaac — the separation of the non-heir sons from the heir echoes the Ishmael expulsion (Genesis 21:14) and anticipates the final separation between those who inherit and those who do not. The gifts given before sending away correspond to God’s common grace: “he causes his sun to rise on the evil and the good” (Matthew 5:45). The gifts are genuine; the separation is real. Both aspects — common blessing to all, particular covenant inheritance to the elect — find their ultimate expression in Christ through whom the world receives common grace and those who believe receive particular redemption (John 3:16–17).
7: Abraham lived a hundred and seventy-five years — the length of Abraham’s life, full of covenant faithfulness (Hebrews 11:8–12). The lifespan functions as a measure of covenantal blessedness: Abraham promised a peaceful death at good old age (Genesis 15:15) and receives it. The patriarchal longevity is the OT sign of blessing; Christ transforms the sign: “Whoever lives by believing in me will never die” (John 11:26). The covenant that Abraham trusted promised long life; the covenant Christ establishes promises eternal life — the antitype infinitely exceeds the type.
8: Abraham breathed his last and died at a good old age, an old man and full of years; and he was gathered to his people — “gathered to his people” implies a corporate afterlife: the dead patriarch joins those who went before. Jesus cites this language in the resurrection debate: “God is not the God of the dead, but of the living” (Matthew 22:32), concluding that Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob are alive before God. Christ’s resurrection confirms what “gathered to his people” assumed: the patriarchs are alive in the presence of God, awaiting the resurrection of the body. The death of Abraham is not the end of Abraham; the one who “believed God” (Genesis 15:6) is alive with the God he trusted.
9: Isaac and Ishmael buried Abraham in the cave of Machpelah — the two sons, covenant and non-covenant, united at their father’s burial. The reunion of the two estranged brothers at their father’s grave prefigures the eschatological gathering of all Abraham’s physical and spiritual children at the resurrection. Christ’s mission to reconcile: “he will gather together in one the scattered children of God” (John 11:52); “God was pleased… through him to reconcile to himself all things” (Colossians 1:19–20). The estrangement that the covenant lines produce in history is overcome at the end; the joint burial at Machpelah is a small anticipation of the final gathering under Christ.
10: The field that Abraham had bought from the Hittites — there Abraham was buried with his wife Sarah — the covenant landholding (Genesis 23) now serves its purpose: it receives the covenant patriarch in death. The burial in the promised land is the final covenant act of faith, planting the dead in the soil of the promise. Hebrews 11:13 says they “admitted that they were foreigners and strangers on earth” — but they buried their dead in the promised land rather than returning to Ur, staking their covenant claim. Christ’s burial in Joseph’s tomb in Jerusalem similarly plants his body in the promised city — from which the resurrection breaks forth. The dead planted in the promise do not stay buried.
11: After Abraham’s death, God blessed his son Isaac, who then lived near Beer Lahai Roi — the covenant blessing passes immediately and completely from Abraham to Isaac. God does not pause or reconsider; the promise does not falter at the generational transition. The uninterrupted transmission of the covenant blessing is the assurance of the gospel’s permanence: “Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever” (Hebrews 13:8). The covenant does not depend on any individual patriarch’s life; it rests in the faithfulness of God who transfers it without loss. Beer Lahai Roi — the “well of the living one who sees me” (Genesis 16:14) — is where Isaac lives, the site of God’s care for Hagar; God’s seeing encompasses Isaac too.
12: This is the account of the family line of Abraham’s son Ishmael, whom Sarah’s slave, Hagar the Egyptian, bore to Abraham — the toledoth (account/genealogy) of Ishmael is placed before the Isaac narrative (v19) to close out the Ishmael line before the covenant story continues. The ordering demonstrates that Ishmael’s line, though real and significant, is not the covenant channel. Paul’s allegory (Galatians 4:22–31) uses precisely this narrative structure: the Hagar/Ishmael line is the “according to the flesh” line that does not receive the inheritance; the Sarah/Isaac line is the “according to the promise” line that does. The genealogical positioning of Ishmael before Isaac signals the closing-off of one line and the continuation of the other.
13: The sons of Ishmael: Nebaioth the firstborn, Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam — the twelve sons of Ishmael parallel the twelve sons of Jacob (Genesis 35:22–26); both men have twelve sons who become twelve tribes or rulers. The twelve-tribe structure runs from Ishmael (twelve rulers, v16) through Jacob (twelve tribes) to the twelve apostles (Matthew 10:2–4), who are the new leadership of the reconstituted covenant people under Christ. Christ appoints twelve “that they might be with him” (Mark 3:14), reconstituting the twelve-tribe structure of Israel in his new community.
14: Mishma, Dumah, Massa — the Ishmael genealogy continues. Dumah appears in Isaiah 21:11 as the oracle against Edom; Massa appears in Proverbs 30:1 (the words of Agur son of Jakeh, from Massa). The genealogical connection of Ishmael’s descendants to the wisdom literature’s fringe figures is a canonical-context note. The wisdom tradition reaches beyond Israel’s borders; Job is from Uz (Job 1:1, connected to Esau’s line), Agur is from Massa. The Spirit who gives wisdom does not respect the covenant boundaries in the same way that the specific covenant blessing does; wisdom literature is open to the wider ANE world.
15: Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish and Kedemah — the remaining sons of Ishmael, completing the twelve. Tema appears in Job 6:19 and Isaiah 21:14 as a desert caravan site. The desert peoples descended from Ishmael are part of the Pauline vision of Galatians: the Ishmael line represents the covenant of law that produces slavery (Galatians 4:25), but the nations descended from Ishmael are not beyond the reach of the gospel. Paul’s Arabia (Galatians 1:17: “I went into Arabia”) is Ishmael’s territory. The very land associated with the non-covenant son becomes the place of Paul’s early preparation for his Gentile mission.
16: These were the sons of Ishmael; these are the names of the twelve tribal rulers according to their settlements and camps — twelve rulers from the non-covenant line, mirroring the structure of the covenant line (12 sons of Jacob). The parallel twelve-ruler structure across the covenant and non-covenant lines shows that God’s common ordering of peoples extends beyond the specific covenant community. The twelve-fold structure is God’s pattern for ordered community; in the new covenant, Christ reconstitutes it through twelve apostles who will “sit on twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel” (Matthew 19:28; Revelation 21:14).
17: Ishmael lived a hundred and thirty-seven years. He breathed his last and died, and was gathered to his people — Ishmael’s death at 137 is recorded with the same “gathered to his people” formula as Abraham (v8). The dignity of the same death-language for the non-covenant son demonstrates God’s care for Ishmael beyond the specific covenant. Christ’s death conquers death for all: “as in Adam all die, so in Christ all will be made alive” (1 Corinthians 15:22). The resurrection hope that Christ secures is not limited to the covenant line; the God who sees Ishmael (Genesis 16:13) does not forget him in death.
18: His descendants settled in the area from Havilah to Shur, near Egypt, toward Assyria — the settlement territory of Ishmael’s descendants corresponds roughly to the Arabian peninsula. The geographic scope of Ishmael’s inheritance — vast but not the promised land — is the type of the distinction between God’s general gifts to all peoples and his specific covenant inheritance secured through Christ. The nations have their appointed territories (Acts 17:26: “From one man he made all the nations… and he marked out their appointed times in history and the boundaries of their lands”); the covenant inheritance transcends national territories and is secured through Christ for all who believe.
19: This is the account of Abraham’s son Isaac. Abraham became the father of Isaac — the new toledoth begins: the Isaac narrative. The double statement “Abraham’s son Isaac — Abraham became the father of Isaac” is deliberate: it asserts the covenant lineage explicitly after the long Ishmael genealogy. The covenant does not drift; it returns to the named channel. Matthew’s genealogy opens with the same insistence on lineage: “This is the genealogy of Jesus the Messiah the son of David, the son of Abraham” (Matthew 1:1). The covenant chain — Abraham to Isaac to Jacob to… to David to… to Christ — is the backbone of salvation history.
20: Isaac was forty years old when he married Rebekah daughter of Bethuel the Aramean — the covenant-heir’s marriage to Rebekah continues the covenant line. The emphasis on Rebekah’s Aramean origin (from Abraham’s extended family, not from Canaan) maintains the covenant community’s genealogical integrity. The great length devoted to finding Rebekah in Genesis 24 signals that the continuation of the covenant line through the right wife is theologically significant. Christ himself comes from the covenant community (“descended from David according to the flesh,” Romans 1:3) — the incarnation is not a random entry into humanity but a specific genealogical embodiment of the covenant promise.
21: Isaac prayed to the LORD on behalf of his wife, because she was childless. The LORD answered his prayer, and Rebekah became pregnant — the barren-wife-and-prayer pattern: Sarah (Genesis 16:1–2; Genesis 21:1–2), Rebekah (here), Rachel (Genesis 30:22), Hannah (1 Samuel 1:19–20), Elizabeth (Luke 1:13). In each case, the covenant-bearing woman’s barrenness is answered by divine action in response to prayer, not by human effort. The sequence of barren women whose wombs God opens culminates in the supreme opening: the virgin Mary conceives by the Holy Spirit (Luke 1:35), bypassing not just infertility but marriage entirely. “Nothing will be impossible with God” (Luke 1:37) is spoken in direct allusion to Sarah’s case (Genesis 18:14) and applies to all the barren-womb promises fulfilled in the one Christ.
22: The babies jostled each other within her, and she said, “Why is this happening to me?” So she went to inquire of the LORD — the prenatal struggle within Rebekah is the starting point of the Jacob-Esau conflict that spans Genesis and beyond. The God who speaks into the prenatal situation is the God who knows persons before their birth: “Before I formed you in the womb I knew you, before you were born I set you apart” (Jeremiah 1:5). Christ, in John 17:24, says the Father loved him “before the creation of the world.” The prenatal divine knowledge of Jacob and Esau is the OT expression of the pre-temporal divine knowledge of persons that the NT applies to both Christ and believers (Ephesians 1:4: “chosen in him before the creation of the world”).
23: The LORD said, “Two nations are in your womb… one people will be stronger than the other, and the older will serve the younger” — Paul’s key election text in Romans 9:10–12: “Yet, before the twins were born or had done anything good or bad — in order that God’s purpose in election might stand: not by works but by him who calls — she was told, ‘The older will serve the younger.’” Paul uses this to demonstrate that God’s election precedes and is independent of human moral performance. The same principle applies to Christ: “chosen before the creation of the world” (1 Peter 1:20); “a chosen and precious cornerstone” (1 Peter 2:6 citing Isaiah 28:16). The God who chooses before birth is the God who chose Christ before creation and who chooses his people in Christ before the foundation of the world (Ephesians 1:4).
24: When the time came for her to give birth, there were twin boys in her womb — the fulfillment of the prophesied twins confirms the divine word of v23. The “when the time came” formula marks the fulfillment of a divine promise within ordinary time — the same formula as Paul’s “when the set time had fully come, God sent his Son” (Galatians 4:4). The birth of the promised child within ordinary time is the pattern of the incarnation: Christ is not a timeless myth but a historical birth “in the fullness of time,” within the flow of ordinary days and months and years. The twin birth fulfills what was announced before birth; the incarnation fulfills what was announced in the garden (Genesis 3:15) and to Abraham (Genesis 22:18).
25: The first to come out was red, and his whole body was like a hairy garment; so they named him Esau — Esau’s red and hairy appearance gives him his name and his later alternate name Edom (“red,” v30). Edom becomes the persistent adversary of Israel throughout the prophets (Isa 34; 63; Obadiah) and the type of the world-power hostile to God’s people. The book of Revelation uses Edom imagery (Isaiah 63:1–6) for the rider on the white horse whose robe is dipped in blood (Revelation 19:13). The figure of Esau/Edom runs from this red appearance in the womb through the OT’s prophetic condemnations to the apocalyptic victory of the Messiah over all adversaries.
26: After this, his brother came out, with his hand grasping Esau’s heel; so he was named Jacob — Jacob grasping the heel at birth (“Jacob” = supplanter / he grasps the heel). Genesis 3:15 promises enmity between the serpent’s offspring and the woman’s offspring, with the serpent striking the heel. Jacob’s heel-grasping from birth is the first heel reference after 3:15 and connects the Jacob narrative to the protoevangelium’s heel-enemy theme. Hosea 12:3 says Jacob “grasped his brother’s heel in the womb.” The struggle from birth is the type of the cosmic struggle between the seed of the woman and the seed of the serpent that Christ resolves by crushing the serpent’s head (Romans 16:20: “the God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet”).
27: The boys grew up, and Esau became a skillful hunter, a man of the open country, while Jacob was content to stay at home among the tents — the contrasting characters of the two sons. The tent-dwelling Jacob mirrors the earlier tent-dwelling of the covenant-bearers (Genesis 12:8; Genesis 18:1; Genesis 25:3). The tent is the sign of the pilgrim who does not put down permanent roots in the current world but awaits the city whose foundations God has laid (Hebrews 11:9–10). Christ himself had no permanent home (Luke 9:58) and calls his disciples into the same pilgrim posture (John 17:16: “they are not of the world, even as I am not of it”). The tent-dweller lives by promise rather than by possession.
28: Isaac, who had a taste for wild game, loved Esau, but Rebekah loved Jacob — the divided parental loyalty creates the family dysfunction that drives the next several chapters. The parental favoritism that distorts covenant family life is the background against which God’s impartial election operates: God chooses Jacob not because Rebekah prefers him but because of divine calling (Romans 9:11). The human distortions of love — favoritism, conditional affection — are the foil against which Christ’s love is defined: “God demonstrates his own love for us in this: While we were still sinners, Christ died for us” (Romans 5:8). Not conditional on our merit or others’ preference, but unconditional in Christ.
29: Once when Jacob was cooking some stew, Esau came in from the open country, famished — the scene of Esau’s fatigue and hunger is the setting for the most consequential bad decision in the patriarchal narrative. The physical hunger that drives Esau to trade his birthright is the type of the momentary desire that the NT warns against: “But each person is tempted when they are dragged away by their own evil desire and enticed. Then, after desire has conceived, it gives birth to sin; and sin, when it is full-grown, gives birth to death” (James 1:14–15). The hunger that feels like death (“I am about to die,” v32) overcomes the covenant sense; Christ’s response to hunger in the wilderness is the opposite: “Man shall not live on bread alone” (Matthew 4:4).
30: “Quick, let me have some of that red stew! I’m famished!” That is why he was also called Edom — Esau’s demand and nickname. The naming of Esau as Edom (“red”) in connection with the red stew is an etiological link between the individual and the nation. The Edomite nation that springs from Esau will be characterized by the same preference for immediate tangible benefit over long-term covenant inheritance. The prophetic condemnations of Edom (Oba 1; Jeremiah 49:7–22; Ezekiel 25:12–14) see in Edom the continuation of Esau’s original disposition: the world-orientation that prefers what is seen over what is promised.
31: Jacob replied, “First sell me your birthright” — Jacob’s shrewdness in recognizing the opportunity and the birthright’s value is itself a morally ambiguous act — exploiting his brother’s weakness. Yet the birthright comes to Jacob, which is what God declared before their birth (v23). The moral complexity of the covenant’s transmission through flawed human agents — Jacob is a supplanter, not a paragon of virtue — is the framework for understanding that God’s covenant purposes are not derailed by human moral failure. Christ does not come through a line of moral heroes but through a line of flawed people whose very failures make his righteousness stand out in greater relief (Matthew 1:1–17 includes Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, and Bathsheba in the genealogy).
32: Esau said, “Look, I am about to die. What good is the birthright to me?” — Esau’s dismissal of the birthright as worthless in the face of present hunger. Hebrews 12:16 names this as the defining characteristic: “godless like Esau, who for a single meal sold his inheritance rights as the oldest son.” The “I am about to die” exaggeration reveals a worldview that maximizes present comfort and discounts future inheritance. The gospel demands the opposite evaluation: “For I consider that the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory that will be revealed in us” (Romans 8:18). The birthright — the covenant inheritance secured by Christ — is worth everything; trading it for any momentary satisfaction is the Esau mistake.
33: Jacob said, “Swear to me first.” So he swore an oath to him, selling his birthright to Jacob — the oath formalizes the transaction. The covenant-oath pattern (swearing by God) is here used for an intra-family transaction that trades a divine inheritance for a bowl of stew. The degradation of the oath-covenant from the solemnity of the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 22:16: God swears by himself) to Esau’s casual oath over a meal represents the covenant’s devaluation by those who do not treasure it. Christ restores the weight and significance of the covenant through his own covenant-ratifying blood (Luke 22:20: “This cup is the new covenant in my blood”), the oath-covenant that cannot be traded or deprecated.
34: Jacob gave Esau bread and lentil stew. He ate and drank, and then got up and left. So Esau despised his birthright — the summary verdict: Esau despised his birthright. The narrator does not soften the judgment. Hebrews 12:16–17 develops this: “Afterward, when he wanted to inherit this blessing, he was rejected. Even though he sought the blessing with tears, he could not change what he had done.” The irrevocability of the despising is the NT’s warning to those who treat lightly the covenant blessings of Christ: “How shall we escape if we ignore so great a salvation?” (Hebrews 2:3). The parable of the pearl of great price (Matthew 13:45–46) presents the right response: the merchant “sold everything he had and bought it.” Esau is the antitype of the true wisdom that treasures the inheritance above all else.
